script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Friday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point eight nine percent up on the session. A strong day across semiconductors and artificial intelligence names drove the gains, with several positions posting outsized moves that deserve a close look.

Advanced Micro Devices surged nearly eight percent on Friday. That number alone tells you something significant shifted in how the market is pricing AI hardware competition — and the catalyst has implications far beyond a single name.

No broad macro data was available for Friday's session. The day's price action was driven almost entirely by company-specific and sector-level developments in artificial intelligence hardware and cloud infrastructure. The Nasdaq Composite posted meaningful gains, with technology and communication services leading the advance while defensive and select growth names lagged.

The dominant story of Friday's session was Advanced Micro Devices, and the catalyst was a development originating at Meta Platforms. Meta launched a new proprietary AI chip, which on the surface might have appeared threatening to third-party semiconductor suppliers. The market read it differently. Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighty-nine cents, up forty dollars and forty-eight cents, a gain of seven point eight two percent on the session. The underlying logic driving that move: Meta's chip announcement was interpreted as confirmation that artificial intelligence compute demand is expanding faster than any single silicon vendor can satisfy. Nvidia rose alongside Advanced Micro Devices for the same reason. What Meta builds internally does not displace merchant silicon — it supplements it, and the aggregate demand signal is unambiguously positive for the broader semiconductor supply chain. Analysts cited across financial media reinforced that read on Friday. For this portfolio, Advanced Micro Devices and Nvidia together represent over eleven percent of holdings. A day where both move sharply higher on the same thesis is a structurally significant session.

Beginning the holdings rundown with the largest position: Nvidia closed at two hundred ten dollars and ninety-six cents, up six dollars and eighty-four cents, a gain of three point three five percent on Friday. The move came directly on the back of the Meta chip headline, with reporting from Yahoo Finance confirming that Meta's new artificial intelligence chip is not designed to replace Nvidia's GPUs — a point that removed overhang from the stock immediately. Additionally, a separate development in the memory market is worth flagging: SK Hynix began trading on the Nasdaq on Friday, a listing that positions it as a key high-bandwidth memory supplier within the same AI hardware ecosystem that Nvidia anchors. That context matters for the supply chain picture around Nvidia's data center business. Watch for any updated commentary from management on HBM allocation heading into next week.

Apple closed at three hundred fifteen dollars and thirty-two cents, up one dollar and ninety-three cents, a gain of zero point six two percent on the session. Yahoo Finance reported Friday that an AI-driven memory shortage is pushing PC shipment volumes lower while simultaneously forcing major technology companies to raise laptop prices. That dynamic creates a mixed read for Apple — unit volume pressure on the hardware side, but potential pricing power that supports margins. The SK Hynix Nasdaq debut, which drew significant coverage on Friday, is directly relevant here as a potential memory supply solution over the medium term. Watch PC pricing trends heading into back-to-school season.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighteen cents, down four dollars and seventy-four cents, a decline of one point three one percent on Friday. Alphabet was among a small number of primary holdings that closed in the red. Coverage from Yahoo Finance flagged that Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle collectively added three hundred fifty billion dollars in debt as part of a combined seven hundred twenty-five billion dollar capital expenditure surge in artificial intelligence infrastructure. Separately, a chart-based analysis highlighted that free cash flow from the AI trade is shifting from hyperscalers toward chipmakers — a headwind for Alphabet's valuation narrative in the near term. Watch for any regulatory developments out of the United Kingdom, where Amazon Web Services was placed under direct financial oversight effective July thirteenth.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred seventy-nine dollars and thirty cents, up thirty dollars and fifty cents, a gain of three point two one percent on Friday. Wealth Management reported Friday that a newly launched memory chip ETF gathered significant assets, reflecting growing institutional appetite for dedicated semiconductor exposure. As the primary pure-play memory name in this portfolio, Micron directly benefits from that capital rotation thesis. Monitor HBM pricing data and any forward guidance revisions tied to the SK Hynix listing, which now sets a more visible public market benchmark for memory valuations.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-five dollars and ten cents, up one dollar and seventy-six cents, a gain of zero point four six percent. No company-specific news drove the move on Friday. Microsoft's modest gain tracked the broader technology sector advance without a distinct catalyst of its own. The artificial intelligence infrastructure spending story that touched Alphabet also implicates Microsoft, given Azure's central role in that buildout. Upcoming earnings commentary will be the next meaningful data point for this position.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and thirty-four cents, up one dollar and seventy-two cents, a gain of zero point seven one percent on Friday. The UK's decision to bring Amazon Web Services under direct financial oversight, effective July thirteenth, introduces a regulatory variable worth monitoring. The practical near-term impact is limited, but the signal — that cloud infrastructure is increasingly being treated as systemically significant by financial regulators — is a trend holders should track. Amazon's position in the AI infrastructure spending surge noted in Friday's reporting also keeps it relevant to the core thesis.

Advanced Micro Devices has already been covered in the top story segment. To briefly restate the close: five hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighty-nine cents, up seven point eight two percent — the strongest single-session performance among all primary holdings on Friday.

Tesla closed at four hundred seven dollars and seventy-six cents, up thirteen dollars and seventy cents, a gain of three point four eight percent on Friday. No company-specific news drove the move. Tesla's gain aligned with a broader risk-on tone in consumer discretionary and high-beta technology names. The stock's correlation to the AI and semiconductor theme continues to influence daily price behavior. Watch for any production or delivery data updates in the coming sessions.

Intel closed at one hundred nine dollars and eighty-four cents, down forty cents, a decline of zero point three six percent on Friday. Intel was the weakest primary holding on the session, closing marginally lower while the rest of the semiconductor complex rallied. The divergence is notable — Intel does not yet have a clear presence in the high-bandwidth memory or AI GPU narrative that drove peers sharply higher on Friday. Upcoming earnings and any product roadmap commentary remain the primary catalysts for this position.

Three data points to track over the next forty-eight hours. First, the United Kingdom's formal activation of financial oversight on Amazon Web Services takes effect July thirteenth — watch for any regulatory statements that could move cloud infrastructure sentiment. Second, monitor SK Hynix's early trading sessions on the Nasdaq following its debut, as its pricing will provide a live benchmark for memory chip valuations directly relevant to Micron. Third, watch for any analyst price target revisions on Advanced Micro Devices and Nvidia following Friday's move — given the size of those positions in this portfolio, post-session research updates will shape Monday's opening tone.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Keep close watch on how the artificial intelligence infrastructure spending debate evolves over the weekend — any new commentary from hyperscalers or chipmakers could set the tone for Monday's open.
